ENGINEERING AND SCIENCE ADMISSIONS TEST
(ESAT)
Content Specification
For assessment in October 2024 and January 2025

children.title: Overview

children.text:
The purpose of the Engineering and Science Admissions Test is to determine a candidate’s
potential to achieve in an academically demanding undergraduate degree course. Questions draw
upon a candidate’s ability to use and apply their scientific and mathematical knowledge. The test is
designed to be challenging in order to differentiate effectively between able applicants, including
those who might have achieved the highest possible grades in school examinations.

children.title: Format

children.text: The test consists of five parts:
Mathematics 1
Biology
Chemistry
Physics

Mathematics 2
Most courses will require candidates to answer Mathematics 1 and two further parts.
Candidates should carefully check the requirements of the course(s) to which they are
applying.
There are 27 multiple-choice questions in each part. The time allowed to complete each part is
40 minutes. Each part is separately timed. If a candidate finishes a part early, the time will not be
carried over to the next part, so candidates should be careful to manage their time accordingly.
The ESAT is a computer-based test. Candidates will be provided with an erasable booklet for
working. Calculators may NOT be used.

children.title: Content

children.text:
Questions will draw upon the topics listed for each part in Appendix 1. All parts will assume
knowledge of the content of Mathematics 1. The knowledge assumed for each part is summarised:
Part Knowledge assumed
Mathematics 1 topics labelled ‘M’ in Appendix 1
Biology topics labelled ‘B’, and topics labelled ‘M’, in Appendix 1
Chemistry topics labelled ‘C’, and topics labelled ‘M’, in Appendix 1
Physics topics labelled ‘P’, and topics labelled ‘M’, in Appendix 1
Mathematics 2 topics labelled ‘M’, and topics labelled ‘MM’, in Appendix 1
Candidates are expected to apply conceptual knowledge to solve problems. Some questions
involve the straightforward application of this knowledge, but others require more creative thinking
and the application of principles in less familiar contexts.

children.title: Scoring

children.text:
In all parts, each correct answer will score 1 mark. No marks are deducted for incorrect answers.
Results for each part will be reported separately.

children.title: Scientific Quantities and Units

children.text:
Throughout this specification, it should be assumed that, where mention is made of a particular
quantity, knowledge of the SI unit of that quantity is also expected (including the relationship of the
unit to other SI units through the equations linking their quantities).
Candidates will be expected to be familiar with the following SI prefixes when used in connection
with any SI unit:
nano- 10–9
micro- 10–6
milli- 10–3
centi- 10–2
deci- 10–1
kilo- 103
mega- 106
giga- 109
Candidates are expected to be familiar with the use of negative indices in units, for example m s–1
for velocity.
The material that follows outlines the scientific and mathematical knowledge assessed in the
Engineering and Science Admissions Test.

children.title: APPENDIX 1: KNOWLEDGE ASSUMED
MATHEMATICS 1

children.text: M1. Units

children.title: Use standard units of mass, length, time, money and other measures.
children.text:
Use compound units such as speed, rates of pay, unit pricing, density and pressure,
including using decimal quantities where appropriate.

children.title: Change freely between related standard units (e.g. time, length, area, volume/capacity,
children.text:
mass) and compound units (e.g. speed, rates of pay, prices, density, pressure) in
numerical and algebraic contexts.
M2. Number

children.title: Order positive and negative integers, decimals and fractions.
children.text: Understand and use the symbols: = , ≠ , < , > , ≤ , ≥ .

children.title: Apply the four operations (addition, subtraction, multiplication and division) to integers,
children.text:
decimals, simple fractions (proper and improper) and mixed numbers – any of which
could be positive and negative.
Understand and use place value.

children.title: Use the concepts and vocabulary of prime numbers, factors (divisors), multiples, common
children.text:
factors, common multiples, highest common factor, lowest common multiple, and prime
factorisation (including use of product notation and the unique factorisation theorem).

children.title: Recognise and use relationships between operations, including inverse operations.
children.text:
Use cancellation to simplify calculations and expressions.
Understand and use the convention for priority of operations, including brackets, powers,
roots and reciprocals.

children.title: Apply systematic listing strategies. (For instance, if there are m ways of doing one task
children.text:
and for each of these tasks there are n ways of doing another task, then the total number
of ways the two tasks can be done in order is m× n ways.)

children.title: Use and understand the terms: square, positive and negative square root, cube and
children.text: cube root.

children.title: Use index laws to simplify numerical expressions, and for multiplication and division of
children.text: integer, fractional and negative powers.

children.title: Interpret, order and calculate with numbers written in standard index form (standard
children.text:
form); numbers are written in standard form as a ×10n, where 1 ≤ a < 10 and n is an
integer.

children.title: Convert between terminating decimals, percentages and fractions.
children.text: Convert between recurring decimals and their corresponding fractions.

children.title: Use fractions, decimals and percentages interchangeably in calculations.
children.text: Understand equivalent fractions.

children.title: Calculate exactly with fractions, surds and multiples of π.
children.text:
Simplify surd expressions involving squares, e.g. 12 = 4 3 × = 4 3 = 2 3 , and
rationalise denominators; for example, candidates could be asked to rationalise
expressions such as: 3
7 , +
3 25 , −
2 3 , −
5 2

children.title: Calculate with upper and lower bounds, and use in contextual problems.
Round numbers and measures to an appropriate degree of accuracy, e.g. to a specified
children.text:
number of decimal places or significant figures.
Use inequality notation to specify simple error intervals due to truncation or rounding.

children.title: Use approximation to produce estimates of calculations, including expressions involving π
children.text:
or surds.
M3. Ratio and proportion

children.title: Understand and use scale factors, scale diagrams and maps.
Express a quantity as a fraction of another, where the fraction is less than 1 or greater
children.text: than 1.

children.title: Understand and use ratio notation.
Divide a given quantity into two (or more) parts in a given part:part ratio.
children.text: Express the division of a quantity into two parts as a ratio.

children.title: Apply ratio to real contexts and problems, such as those involving conversion,
children.text:
comparison, scaling, mixing and concentrations.
Express a multiplicative relationship between two quantities as a ratio or a fraction.

children.title: Understand and use proportion.
children.text: Relate ratios to fractions and to linear functions.

children.title: Identify and work with fractions in ratio problems.
Define percentage as ‘number of parts per hundred’.
children.text:
Interpret percentages and percentage changes as a fraction or a decimal, and interpret
these multiplicatively.
Express one quantity as a percentage of another.
Compare two quantities using percentages.
Work with percentages greater than 100%.
Solve problems involving percentage change, including percentage increase/decrease,
original value problems and simple interest calculations.

children.title: Understand and use direct and inverse proportion, including algebraic representations.
children.text:
Recognise and interpret graphs that illustrate direct and inverse proportion.
Set up, use and interpret equations to solve problems involving direct and inverse
proportion (including questions involving integer and fractional powers).
Understand that x is inversely proportional to y is equivalent to x is proportional to
y
1 .

children.title: Compare lengths, areas and volumes using ratio notation.
children.text: Understand and make links to similarity (including trigonometric ratios) and scale factors.

children.title: Set up, solve and interpret the answers in growth and decay problems, including
children.text:
compound interest, and work with general iterative processes.
M4. Algebra

children.title: Understand, use and interpret algebraic notation; for instance: ab in place of a×b ;
Use index laws in algebra for multiplication and division of integer, fractional, and
children.text: negative powers.

children.title: Substitute numerical values into formulae and expressions, including scientific formulae.
children.text:
Understand and use the concepts and vocabulary: expressions, equations, formulae,
identities, inequalities, terms and factors.

children.title: Collect like terms, multiply a single term over a bracket, take out common factors, and
children.text: expand products of two or more binomials.

children.title: Factorise quadratic expressions of the form x2 +bx +c, including the difference of two
children.text:
squares.
Factorise quadratic expressions of the form ax2 +bx +c, including the difference of two
squares.

children.title: Simplify expressions involving sums, products and powers, including the laws of indices.
children.text:
Simplify rational expressions by cancelling, or factorising and cancelling.
Use the four rules on algebraic rational expressions.

children.title: Rearrange formulae to change the subject.
Understand the difference between an equation and an identity.
children.text: Argue mathematically to show that algebraic expressions are equivalent.

children.title: Work with coordinates in all four quadrants.
Identify and interpret gradients and intercepts of linear functions ( y = mx + c ) graphically
children.text:
and algebraically.
Identify pairs of parallel lines and identify pairs of perpendicular lines, including the
relationships between gradients.
Find the equation of the line through two given points, or through one point with a given
gradient.

children.title: Identify and interpret roots, intercepts and turning points of quadratic functions
children.text:
graphically.
Deduce roots algebraically, and turning points by completing the square.

children.title: Recognise, sketch and interpret graphs of:

children.label: a
children.text: linear functions

children.label: b
children.text: quadratic functions

children.label: c
children.text: simple cubic functions

children.label: d
children.text:
the reciprocal function: y = x
1 with x≠0

children.label: e
children.text:
the exponential function: y = kx
for positive values of k

children.label: f
children.text:
trigonometric functions (with arguments in degrees): y = sin x, y = cos x, y = tanx
for angles of any size

children.title: Interpret graphs (including reciprocal graphs and exponential graphs) and graphs of
children.text:
non-standard functions in real contexts to find approximate solutions to problems, such as
simple kinematic problems involving distance, speed and acceleration.

children.title: Calculate or estimate gradients of graphs and areas under graphs (including quadratic
children.text:
and other non-linear graphs), and interpret results in cases such as distance–time graphs,
speed–time graphs and graphs in financial contexts.

children.title: Set up and solve, both algebraically and graphically, simple equations including
children.text:
simultaneous equations involving two unknowns; this may include one linear and one
quadratic equation.
Solve two simultaneous equations in two variables (linear/linear or linear/quadratic)
algebraically.
Find approximate solutions using a graph.
Translate simple situations or procedures into algebraic expressions or formulae; for
example, derive an equation (or two simultaneous equations), solve the equation(s) and
interpret the solution.

children.title: Solve quadratic equations (including those that require rearrangement) algebraically by
children.text:
factorising, by completing the square, and by using the quadratic formula.
Know the quadratic formula:
a
b b ac
x
4 2 − ± − =
Find approximate solutions of quadratic equations using a graph.

children.title: Solve linear inequalities in one or two variables.
children.text: Represent the solution set on a number line, or on a graph, or in words.

children.title: Generate terms of a sequence using term-to-term or position-to-term rules.
Deduce expressions to calculate the nth term of linear or quadratic sequences.
children.text: M5. Geometry

children.title: Use conventional terms and notation: points, lines, line segments, vertices, edges,
children.text:
planes, parallel lines, perpendicular lines, right angles, subtended angles, polygons,
regular polygons and polygons with reflection and/or rotational symmetries.

children.title: Recall and use the properties of angles at a point, angles on a straight line, perpendicular
children.text:
lines and opposite angles at a vertex.
Understand and use the angle properties of parallel lines, intersecting lines, triangles and
quadrilaterals.
Calculate and use the sum of the interior angles, and the sum of the exterior angles, of
polygons.

children.title: Derive and apply the properties and definitions of special types of quadrilaterals, including
children.text:
square, rectangle, parallelogram, trapezium, kite and rhombus.
Derive and apply the properties and definitions of various types of triangle and other
plane figures using appropriate language.

children.title: Understand and use the basic congruence criteria for triangles (SSS, SAS, ASA, RHS).
Apply angle facts, triangle congruence, similarity, and properties of quadrilaterals to
children.text: results about angles and sides.

children.title: Identify, describe and construct congruent and similar shapes, including on coordinate
children.text:
axes, by considering rotation, reflection, translation and enlargement (including fractional
and negative scale factors).
Describe the changes and invariance achieved by combinations of rotations, reflections
and translations.
Describe translations as 2-dimensional vectors.

children.title: Know and use the formula for Pythagoras’ theorem: a2 + b2 = c2
children.text: Use Pythagoras’ theorem in both 2 and 3 dimensions.

children.title: Identify and use conventional circle terms: centre, radius, chord, diameter, circumference,
children.text:
tangent, arc, sector and segment (including the use of the terms minor and major for arcs,
sectors and segments).

children.title: Apply the standard circle theorems concerning angles, radii, tangents and chords, and

children.label: a
children.text: angle subtended at the centre is twice the angle subtended at the circumference

children.label: b
children.text: angle in a semicircle is 90°

children.label: c
children.text: angles in the same segment are equal

children.label: d
children.text: angle between a tangent and a chord (alternate segment theorem)

children.label: e
children.text: angle between a radius and a tangent is 90°

children.label: f
children.text: properties of cyclic quadrilaterals
use them to prove related results:

children.title: Solve geometrical problems on 2-dimensional coordinate axes.
Know the terminology faces, surfaces, edges and vertices when applied to cubes,
children.text: cuboids, prisms, cylinders, pyramids, cones, spheres and hemispheres.

children.title: Interpret plans and elevations of 3-dimensional shapes.
Use and interpret maps and scale drawings.
children.text: Understand and use three-figure bearings.

children.title: Know and apply formulae to calculate:

children.label: a
children.text: the area of triangles, parallelograms, trapezia

children.label: b
children.text: the volume of cuboids and other right prisms.

children.title: Know the formulae:

children.label: a
children.text: circumference of a circle = 2πr = πd

children.label: b
children.text: area of a circle = πr2

children.label: c
children.text:
volume of a right circular cylinder = πr2h
Formulae relating to spheres, pyramids and cones will be given if needed.
Use formulae to calculate:

children.label: a
children.text: perimeters of 2-dimensional shapes, including circles

children.label: b
children.text: areas of circles and composite shapes

children.label: c
children.text: surface area and volume of spheres, pyramids, cones and composite solids

children.title: Calculate arc lengths, angles and areas of sectors of circles.
Apply the concepts of congruence and similarity in simple figures, including the
children.text: relationships between lengths, areas and volumes.

children.title: Know and use the trigonometric ratios:
children.text:
sinθ = hypotenuse
opposite cosθ = hypotenuse
adjacent tanθ = adjacent
opposite
Apply these to find angles and lengths in right-angled triangles and, where possible,
general triangles in 2- and 3-dimensional figures.
Know the exact values of sinθ and cosθ for θ = 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°, 90°.
Know the exact values of tanθ for θ = 0°, 30°, 45°, 60°.
Candidates are not expected to recall or use the sine or cosine rules.

children.title: Apply addition and subtraction of vectors, multiplication of vectors by a scalar, and
children.text:
diagrammatic and column representations of vectors.
Use vectors to construct geometric arguments and proofs.
M6. Statistics

children.title: Interpret and construct tables, charts and diagrams, including:

children.label: a
children.text:
two-way tables, frequency tables, bar charts, pie charts and pictograms for
categorical data

children.label: b
children.text: vertical line charts for ungrouped discrete numerical data

children.label: c
children.text:
tables and line graphs for time series data
Know the appropriate use of each of these representations.

children.title: Interpret and construct diagrams for grouped discrete data and continuous data:

children.label: a
children.text: histograms with equal and unequal class intervals

children.label: b
children.text:
cumulative frequency graphs
Know the appropriate use of each of these diagrams.
Understand and use the term frequency density.

children.title: Calculate the mean, mode, median and range for ungrouped data.
children.text:
Find the modal class; calculate estimates of the range, mean and median for grouped
data, and understand why these are estimates.
Describe a population using statistics.
Make simple comparisons.
Compare data sets using like-for-like summary values.
Understand the advantages and disadvantages of summary values.
Calculate estimates of mean, median, mode, range, quartiles and interquartile range from
graphical representation of grouped data.
Use the median and interquartile range to compare distributions.

children.title: Use and interpret scatter graphs of bivariate data.
children.text:
Recognise correlation, and know that it does not indicate causation.
Draw estimated lines of best fit.
Interpolate and extrapolate apparent trends whilst knowing the dangers of so doing.
M7. Probability

children.title: Analyse the frequency of outcomes of probability experiments using tables and frequency
children.text: trees.

children.title: Apply ideas of randomness, fairness and equally likely events to calculate expected
children.text:
outcomes of multiple future experiments.
Understand that if an experiment is repeated, the outcome may be different.

children.title: Relate relative expected frequencies to theoretical probability, using appropriate language
children.text: and the ‘0 to 1’ probability scale.

children.title: Apply the property that the probabilities of an exhaustive set of outcomes sum to one.
children.text:
Apply the property that the probabilities of an exhaustive set of mutually exclusive events
sum to one.

children.title: Enumerate sets and combinations of sets systematically, using tables, grids, Venn
children.text:
diagrams and tree diagrams. Candidates are not expected to know formal set theory
notation.

children.title: Construct theoretical possibility spaces for single and combined experiments with equally
children.text: likely outcomes, and use these to calculate theoretical probabilities.

children.title: Know when to add or multiply two probabilities, and understand conditional probability.

children.label: a
children.text: when the probabilities are independent of the previous outcome

children.label: b
children.text: when the probabilities are dependent on the previous outcome.

Calculate and interpret conditional probabilities through representation using expected
frequencies with two-way tables, tree diagrams and Venn diagrams.
Understand the use of tree diagrams to represent outcomes of combined events:

children.title: BIOLOGY

children.text: B1. Cells

children.title: Know and understand the structure and function of the main sub-cellular components of

children.label: a
children.text: cell membrane

children.label: b
children.text: cytoplasm

children.label: c
children.text: nucleus

children.label: d
children.text: mitochondrion

children.label: e
children.text: cell wall (plant only)

children.label: f
children.text: chloroplast (plant only)

children.label: g
children.text: vacuole (plant only)
eukaryotic cells (both animal and plant) including:

children.title: Know and understand the structure and function of the main sub-cellular components of

children.label: a
children.text: cell membrane

children.label: b
children.text: cytoplasm

children.label: c
children.text: cell wall

children.label: d
children.text: chromosomal DNA/no ‘true’ nucleus

children.label: e
children.text: plasmid DNA
prokaryotic cells (bacteria) including:

children.title: Know and understand the levels of organisation within organisms as: cells to tissues to
children.text:
organs to organ systems.
B2. Movement across membranes

children.title: Know and understand the processes of diffusion, osmosis (in terms of water potential),
children.text:
and active transport, including examples in living and non-living systems.
B3. Cell division and sex determination

children.title: Mitosis and the cell cycle:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand that the mitotic cell cycle includes interphase (involving
cell growth and DNA replication) and mitosis (involving one cell division leading
to two daughter cells which are genetically identical to each other and to the
parental cell).

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand the importance of mitosis in the growth of an organism:
specifically, its roles in increasing the number of cells, repairing tissues, replacing
cells and asexual reproduction.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand that cancer is the result of changes in cells, including
mutations, that lead to uncontrolled cell division.

children.title: Meiosis and the cell cycle:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand that the meiotic cell cycle includes interphase (involving
cell growth and DNA replication) and meiosis (involving two cell divisions leading
to four daughter cells, each with a single copy of each chromosome).

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand the role of meiosis in producing genetically different
haploid gametes so that the zygote (fertilised egg cell) produced at fertilisation is
diploid.

children.title: Asexual and sexual reproduction:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand that asexual reproduction involves one parent and that
offspring are genetically identical when no mutations occur.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand that sexual reproduction involves two parents and that
offspring are genetically different in relation to each other and the parents,
leading to (increased) variation.

children.title: Sex determination:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know that, in most mammals including humans, females are XX and males
are XY.

children.label: b
children.text:
Analyse genetic data and diagrams to establish the sex and ratio of offspring.
B4. Inheritance

children.title: Know and understand the nucleus as a site of genetic material in eukaryotic cells.
Know and understand the following genetic terms:

children.label: a
children.text: gene

children.label: b
children.text: allele

children.label: c
children.text: dominant

children.label: d
children.text: recessive

children.label: e
children.text: heterozygous

children.label: f
children.text: homozygous

children.label: g
children.text: phenotype

children.label: h
children.text: genotype

children.label: i
children.text: chromosome

children.label: j
children.text: autosome

children.title: Monohybrid crosses:

children.label: a
children.text:
Use and interpret genetic data and diagrams involving monohybrid (single gene)
crosses.

children.label: b
children.text:
Use and interpret family trees/pedigrees and express outcomes as ratios,
numbers, probabilities or percentages.

children.label: c
children.text: Understand the concept of inherited conditions.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know that most phenotypes are the result of multiple genes and only some result
from single gene inheritance.
B5. DNA

children.title: Know and understand that

children.label: a
children.text: the genome is the full set of genetic material (DNA) of an organism.

children.label: b
children.text: this DNA is contained within chromosomes.

children.title: Know and understand the structure of DNA:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand that single-stranded DNA (ssDNA) is a polymer made up
of nucleotides joined together to form one strand of DNA.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand that double-stranded DNA (dsDNA) is a polymer made up
of two strands of DNA forming a double helix.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand that the structure of each nucleotide consists of a common
sugar and phosphate group as well as one of four different nitrogenous bases.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know the complementary pairs of DNA nitrogenous bases – adenine (A) pairs
with thymine (T) and guanine (G) pairs with cytosine (C) – and that the sequence
of these bases is the genetic code.

children.title: Protein synthesis:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand that protein synthesis involves producing chains of amino
acids called polypeptides.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand that one or more polypeptide(s) can form a functional
protein.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand that the three-dimensional shape of a protein is
determined by the sequence of its amino acids.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know and understand that the sequence of nucleotide bases in a gene
determines the sequence of amino acids in the polypeptide the gene codes for.

children.label: e
children.text:
Know and understand that the sequence of nucleotides in a gene is ‘read’ as
triplets, and that each triplet codes for an amino acid.

children.title: Gene mutations:

children.label: a
children.text: Understand that a mutation changes the sequence of nucleotides in the DNA.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know that most mutations have no effect on the phenotype, some will have a
small effect, whilst occasionally others will determine the phenotype.
B6. Gene technologies

children.title: Genetic engineering:

children.label: a
children.text: Understand the process of genetic engineering to include:

children.label: i
children.text: taking a copy of a gene from the DNA of one organism

children.label: ii
children.text: insertion of that gene into the DNA of another organism

children.label: iii
children.text: the roles of restriction enzymes and ligases in recombining DNA.

children.label: b
children.text: Recall and interpret examples of genetic engineering in different cell types.

children.label: c
children.text:
Explain the benefits and risks of using genetic engineering in medical
applications.

children.title: Stem cells:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand that some early embryonic cells are totipotent and have
the potential to develop into a complete multicellular organism.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand that most embryonic stem cells are pluripotent and can
differentiate into any cell type.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand that adult stem cells are multipotent and can differentiate
into a limited number of different cell types.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know and understand the likely benefits and risks of using stem cells in medical
applications.

children.title: Selective breeding:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand the differences and similarities between natural selection
and selective breeding.

children.label: b
children.text:
Understand the impact of selective breeding on populations.
B7. Variation

children.title: Natural selection and evolution:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know that there is usually extensive genetic variation within a population of a
species.

children.label: b
children.text:
Describe evolution as a change in the inherited characteristics of a population
over time through a process of natural selection which may result in the
formation of a new species.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand how evolution can occur through natural selection of
variants that give rise to phenotypes best suited to their environment.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know and understand that antibiotic resistance in bacteria is an example of
evolution through natural selection.

children.title: Sources of variation:

children.label: a
children.text:
Understand that variation can be genetic/inherited, resulting in a range of
phenotypes.

children.label: b
children.text:
Understand that variation can also be environmental, which affects a range of
phenotypes.
B8. Enzymes

children.title: Know and understand that enzymes are primarily proteins that function as biological
children.text: catalysts.

children.title: Know and understand the general mechanism of enzyme action, including the role of the
children.text: active site and enzyme specificity.

children.title: Know and understand how the factors of temperature and pH can affect the rate of
children.text: enzyme action.

children.title: Know the role of amylases, proteases and lipases in the digestion of carbohydrates,
children.text:
proteins and fats.
B9. Animal physiology

children.title: Respiration:

children.label: a
children.text: Know and understand the process of cellular respiration in living cells.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand the process of aerobic respiration in living cells, including
the word equation.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand the process of anaerobic respiration in animal cells,
including the word equation.

children.title: Organ systems:

children.label: a
children.text: Nervous system:

children.label: i
children.text:
Know and understand that the central nervous system comprises the brain
and spinal cord.

children.label: ii
children.text:
Know and understand the structure and function of sensory neurones,
relay neurones, motor neurones, synapses and the reflex arc.

children.label: b
children.text: Respiratory system:

children.label: i
children.text:
Know and understand the structure and function of the respiratory
(breathing) system, including the structure of the thorax.

children.label: ii
children.text: Understand the processes of ventilation and gas exchange.

children.label: iii
children.text:
Know and understand the importance of a high surface area: volume ratio
for the gas exchange process.

children.label: c
children.text: Circulatory system:

children.label: i
children.text:
Know and understand the structure and function of the circulatory system,
including the structure of the heart, characteristics of heart rate, ECGs, and
the main types of blood vessel (arteries, veins and capillaries).

children.label: ii
children.text:
Know and understand the composition and function of the blood (red blood
cells carry oxygen; white blood cells are involved in antibody production
and phagocytosis; platelets are involved in blood clotting; and plasma is
involved both in the transport of blood components and other dissolved
substances including hormones, antibodies, urea and carbon dioxide, and
in the distribution of heat).

children.label: iii
children.text: Know and understand the relationship with the gaseous exchange system.

children.label: iv
children.text:
Know and understand the need for exchange surfaces and a transport
system in multicellular organisms in terms of surface area: volume ratio.

children.label: d
children.text: Digestive system:

children.label: i
children.text: Know and understand the structure and function of the digestive system.

children.label: ii
children.text:
Know and understand the processes of peristalsis, digestion, absorption
and egestion.

children.label: e
children.text: Excretory system:

children.label: i
children.text:
Know and understand the structure and function of the excretory system,
including the kidney and the main components of the nephron.

children.label: ii
children.text: Understand the role of the kidneys in homeostasis.

children.title: Homeostasis:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know that homeostasis is the maintenance of a constant internal environment,
and appreciate its importance in multicellular animals.

children.label: b
children.text: Understand the concept of negative feedback in the context of homeostasis.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand the regulation of blood glucose levels, including the role of
insulin and glucagon.

children.label: d
children.text:
Understand the main features of type 1 and type 2 diabetes, and how type 1
diabetes can be treated.

children.label: e
children.text:
Know and understand the regulation of water content (including the role of ADH)
and the regulation of body temperature.

children.title: Hormones:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand that hormones are released from specific endocrine
glands and travel via the blood to their target structures.

children.label: b
children.text: Know and understand the main role of adrenaline in the body.

children.label: c
children.text: Know and understand the roles of hormones in human reproduction including:

children.label: i
children.text:
those involved in the menstrual cycle (FSH, LH, oestrogen and
progesterone).

children.label: ii
children.text:
those used for contraception, and the differences between hormonal and
non-hormonal forms of contraception.

children.title: Disease and body defence:

children.label: a
children.text: Communicable diseases:

children.label: i
children.text:
Know that communicable diseases are caused by pathogenic bacteria,
viruses, protists and fungi.

children.label: ii
children.text:
Understand the transmission routes of sexually transmitted infections,
including the effect on the immune system of HIV which results in AIDS.

children.label: iii
children.text:
Understand the treatment of disease, including the use of antibiotics,
vaccines (role of dead and inactive pathogens, antibody production and
formation of memory cells) and techniques to prevent the spread of
pathogens including HIV.

children.label: iv
children.text:
Understand the process of discovery and development of new medicines
and vaccines, including pre-clinical and clinical testing.

children.label: b
children.text: Non-communicable diseases:

children.label: i
children.text:
Know that the following diseases are caused by the interaction of many
factors: cardiovascular disease, many forms of cancer, some lung and liver
diseases and diseases influenced by nutrition, including type 2 diabetes.

children.label: ii
children.text:
Know that cardiovascular disease can be treated/managed using lifelong
medication (including statins, anti-coagulants and anti-hypertensive drugs),
surgical procedures (including stents and bypass for coronary heart
disease), and lifestyle changes (including reducing smoking, more exercise
and a balanced diet).
B10. Ecosystems

children.title: Levels of organisation in an ecosystem:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand the organisation of levels within an ecosystem from
individuals through to populations, and from communities through to ecosystems.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand how communities can be affected by abiotic and biotic
factors.

children.label: c
children.text: Know and understand the factors that can cause a population to change in size.

children.label: d
children.text:
Understand the importance of interdependence in ecosystems (relating to
predation, mutualism and parasitism) and of competition in a community.

children.label: e
children.text:
Know and understand that photosynthetic organisms are the primary producers
of food in an ecosystem, and therefore biomass.

children.title: Material cycling:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand the carbon cycle, including the importance of the following
processes:

children.label: i
children.text: photosynthesis

children.label: ii
children.text: respiration

children.label: iii
children.text: combustion

children.label: iv
children.text: decomposition

children.label: b
children.text: Understand the importance of the water cycle to living organisms.

children.title: Biodiversity:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand how quadrats and belt transects are used to investigate
the distribution and abundance of organisms in a habitat, and interpret data from
their use.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand how to determine the number of organisms in a given
area.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand the positive and negative human interactions in an
ecosystem, including fish farming, acid rain and eutrophication, and explain their
impact on biodiversity.
B11. Plant physiology

children.title: Importance of photosynthesis:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand the process of photosynthesis as an endothermic reaction
that uses light energy to react carbon dioxide and water to produce glucose and
oxygen.

children.label: b
children.text:
Understand the effect of temperature, light intensity and carbon dioxide
concentration as limiting factors on the rate of photosynthesis.

children.title: Transport systems in plants:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand how the structures of xylem and phloem are adapted to
their functions in plants, including the role of:

children.label: i
children.text:
lignified dead cells in xylem tissue in the transport of water and mineral
ions from the roots to the stems and leaves.

children.label: ii
children.text:
living cells in phloem tissue in the transport of dissolved sugars from the
leaves to the rest of the plant for immediate use or storage.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know and understand how water and mineral ions are taken up by plants, and
relate the structure of root hair cells to their function in this.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand the processes of transpiration and translocation, including
the structure and function of the stomata.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know and understand the effect of environmental factors on the rate of water
uptake by a plant, including light intensity, air movement, humidity and
temperature.

children.label: e
children.text:
Calculate the rate of transpiration as:
rate of transpiration =
volume of water
time taken

children.title: CHEMISTRY

children.text: C1. Atomic structure

children.title: Describe the structure of the atom as a central nucleus (containing protons and neutrons)
children.text: surrounded by electrons moving in shells/energy levels.

children.title: Know the relative masses and charges of protons, neutrons and electrons, and recognise
children.text: that most of the mass of an atom is in the nucleus.

children.title: Know and be able to use the terms atomic number and mass number, together with
children.text:
standard notation (e.g. C12
6 ), and so be able to calculate the number of protons, neutrons
and electrons in any atom or ion.

children.title: Use the atomic number to write the electron configurations of the first 20 elements in the
children.text: Periodic Table (H to Ca) in comma-separated format (e.g. 2,8,8,1 for a potassium atom).

children.title: Know the definition of isotopes as atoms of an element with the same number of protons
children.text:
but different numbers of neutrons (so having different mass numbers). Use data, including
that from a mass spectrometer, to identify the number and abundances of different
isotopes of elements.

children.title: Know and use the concept of relative atomic mass, Ar, including calculating values from
children.text:
given data.
C2. The Periodic Table (IUPAC conventions, Groups are labelled as 1-18)

children.title: Know that Periods are horizontal rows and Groups are vertical columns.
Know that the elements are arranged in the order of increasing atomic number.
Recall the position of metals and non-metals in the Periodic Table: alkali metals
children.text:
(Group 1), alkaline earth metals (Group 2), common non-metals in Group 16, the
halogens (Group 17), the noble gases (Group 18) and the transition metals.

children.title: Know and use the relationship between the position of an atom in the Periodic Table
children.text: (Group and Period) and the electron configuration of the atom.

children.title: Understand that elements in the same Group have similar chemical properties and that
children.text:
down a metal Group, reactivity increases and down a non-metal Group, reactivity
decreases.
C3. Chemical reactions, formulae and equations

children.title: Understand that in a chemical reaction, new substances are formed by the rearrangement
children.text: of atoms and their electrons, but no nuclei are destroyed or created.

children.title: Know the chemical formulae of simple, common ionic and covalent compounds.
Know and use state symbols: solid (s), liquid (l), gas (g), aqueous solution (aq).
Be able to construct and balance a chemical equation, including ionic and half-equations.
Understand that often chemical reactions can be reversible and do not go to completion.

children.label: a
children.text:
Know the factors that can affect the position of an equilibrium (concentration of
reactants/products, temperature, overall pressure).

children.label: b
children.text:
Predict the effect of changing these factors on the position of equilibrium.
C4. Quantitative chemistry

All of the reactants do not turn fully into the products but the reaction reaches a state of
equilibrium in a closed system.

children.title: Use Ar values to calculate the relative molar mass, Mr.
Know that Avogadro’s number gives the number of particles in one mole of a substance.
Know that one mole of a substance is the Ar or Mr in grams, and perform conversions of
children.text:
grams to moles and vice versa (including working in tonnes and kilograms). Know that the
amount of a substance corresponds to the number of moles of a substance.

children.title: Calculate the percentage composition by mass of a compound using given Ar values.
Know that the empirical formula is the simplest integer ratio of atoms in a compound. Find
children.text:
the empirical formula of a compound from a variety of data, such as the percentage
composition by mass of the elements present or reacting masses. Find the molecular
formula from the empirical formula if given the Mr value.

children.title: Use balanced chemical equations to calculate the masses of reactants and products,
children.text: including if there is a limiting reactant present.

children.title: Be able to construct balanced chemical equations from reacting masses or gas volumes
children.text: data.

children.title: Understand that (for an ideal gas) one mole of a gas occupies a set volume at a given
children.text:
temperature and pressure (for example, 24 dm3 at room temperature and pressure (rtp)),
and perform conversions of volumes to number of moles, and vice versa.

children.title: Solutions:

children.label: a
children.text:
Understand that concentration can be measured in mol dm–3 or g dm–3, and be
able to calculate the concentration given the number of moles (or mass) of solute
and the volume of solution.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know the term saturated solution, be able to calculate solubility and interpret
solubility data.

children.title: Use the concentrations of solutions (or find the concentrations from given data) and the
children.text: reacting ratio of reactants from the balanced equation to perform titration calculations.

children.title: Calculate the percentage yield of a reaction using the balanced chemical equation and
children.text:
the equation: percentage yield=
predicted yield (g)
actual yield (g) ×100
C5. Oxidation, reduction and redox

children.title: Know that on a basic level, oxidation is the gain of oxygen and that reduction is the
children.text: removal of oxygen.

children.title: Know and be able to use the concept that oxidation and reduction are the transfer of
children.text: electrons, i.e. reduction is the gain of electrons and oxidation is the loss of electrons.

children.title: Determine and use the oxidation states of atoms in simple inorganic compounds.
Identify any chemical equation that involves: oxidation only, reduction only, redox (both
children.text: oxidation and reduction taking place), or no oxidation/reduction.

children.title: Understand the concept of disproportionation and recognise reactions (or species) where
children.text: this occurs.

children.title: Understand the terms oxidising agent and reducing agent, and be able to identify them in
children.text:
reactions.
C6. Chemical bonding, structure and properties

children.title: Define and understand the differences between elements, compounds and mixtures.
Understand that atoms often react to form compounds which have the electron
children.text:
configuration of a noble gas (Group 18). Understand that the type of bonding taking place
depends on the atoms involved in the reaction.

children.title: Ionic bonding:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know that ions are formed by transfer of electrons from atoms of metals to atoms
of non-metals, and that these ions (of opposite charge) attract to form ionic
compounds.

children.label: b
children.text:
Predict the charge of the most stable ions formed from elements in Groups 1, 2,
16 and 17 and aluminium by consideration of their electron configuration.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know the chemical formulae of common compound ions, e.g. CO3
2– and OH–.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know that when an element can exist in more than one oxidation state, e.g. Cu,
Fe, then Roman numerals are used to denote the one present, e.g. iron(III)
chloride for FeCl3.

children.label: e
children.text: Determine the formulae of ionic compounds from their constituent ions.

children.label: f
children.text:
Understand the general physical properties of ionic compounds, such as melting
point and conductivity.

children.title: Covalent bonding:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know that a covalent bond is formed when atoms share one (or more) pair(s) of
electrons, generally between non-metals.

children.label: b
children.text:
Understand that covalently bonded substances can be small molecules (e.g.
water, ammonia, methane) or giant structures (e.g. diamond, graphite, silicon
dioxide).

children.label: c
children.text:
Understand the general physical properties of substances composed of small
molecules or of those that exist as giant covalent structures.

children.title: Metallic bonding:

children.label: a
children.text:
Understand that solid metals exist as a giant structure of positively charged ions
surrounded by delocalised (free) electrons.

children.label: b
children.text:
Understand the general physical properties of metals, such as melting point and
conductivity.

children.title: Understand that intermolecular forces can exist between molecules, and that these forces
children.text: must be overcome in melting and boiling.

children.title: Be able to relate structure and bonding to physical properties, such as melting point and
children.text:
conductivity.
C7. Group chemistry

children.title: Know the physical and chemical properties of the alkali metals (Group 1), the halogens
children.text: (Group 17) and the noble gases (Group 18).

children.title: Describe the trends in chemical reactivity and physical properties of the alkali metals
children.text:
(Group 1) and make predictions based on those trends. This includes knowledge of the
relative positions of lithium, sodium and potassium in Group 1.

children.title: The halogens (Group 17):

children.label: a
children.text:
Describe the trends in chemical reactivity and physical properties of the halogens
and make predictions based on those trends. This includes knowledge of the
relative positions of fluorine, chlorine, bromine and iodine in Group 17.

children.label: b
children.text:
Explain what is meant by a displacement reaction, in terms of reactivity
competition, between halogens and halide ions.
C8. Separation techniques

children.title: Know that chemical processes are required to displace constituent elements from their
children.text: compounds.

children.title: Know that physical processes are required to separate mixtures, including
children.text: miscible/immiscible liquids and dissolved/insoluble solids.

children.title: Know when to apply the following separation techniques: simple/fractional distillation,
children.text:
paper chromatography (including use of Rf values), use of a separating funnel,
centrifugation, dissolving, filtration, evaporation and crystallisation.

children.title: Know how to establish the purity of a substance using chromatography.
children.text: C9. Acids, bases and salts

children.title: Acids:

children.label: a
children.text: Define an acid as a substance that can form H+(aq) ions or that is an H+ donor.

children.label: b
children.text:
Describe reactions with metals, carbonates, metal hydroxides and metal oxides
in which salts are formed.

children.label: c
children.text: Understand the terms strong, weak, dilute and concentrated.

children.label: d
children.text: Know that some oxides of non-metals react with water to form acidic solutions.

children.label: e
children.text:
Recall that pH is a measure of H+ ion concentration, and recall that a change of 1
on the pH scale corresponds to a change by a factor of 10 in H+ ion
concentration.

children.label: f
children.text:
Know that one mole of some acidic substances is able to form/donate more than
one mole of H+ ions, including the use of the terms mono-, di-, tri-, and polyprotic.

children.title: Bases:

children.label: a
children.text:
Define a base as a substance that can form OH–(aq) ions or that is an H+
acceptor.

children.label: b
children.text: Understand the terms strong, weak, dilute and concentrated.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know that some oxides and hydroxides of metals react with water to form
alkaline solutions.

children.title: Know that the reaction of an acid with a base can lead to neutralisation and is often
children.text:
exothermic.
C10. Rates of reaction

children.title: Describe the qualitative effects on a rate of reaction of concentration, temperature,
children.text: particle size, a catalyst and, for gases, pressure.

children.title: Know that the rate of reaction can be found by measuring the loss of a reactant or the
children.text:
gain of a product, or by measurement of a physical property over time, and be able to
identify which of these measurements can be used in a given situation.

children.title: Interpret data in graphical form concerning the rate of a reaction.
Use collision theory to explain changes in the rate of a reaction.
Understand that particles must have sufficient energy when they collide to react, and that
children.text:
this energy is called the activation energy (Ea). Identify the activation energy on an energy
level diagram.

children.title: Know that catalysts:

children.label: a
children.text: are not used up in a reaction.

children.label: b
children.text: are chemically unchanged at the end of a reaction.

children.label: c
children.text:
provide an alternative route (reaction mechanism) with a lower activation energy,
and interpret this effect on an energy level diagram.

children.label: d
children.text:
do not affect the position of an equilibrium.
C11. Energetics

children.title: Understand the concepts of an exothermic reaction, for which ΔH is negative (negative
children.text:
enthalpy change), and an endothermic reaction, for which ΔH is positive (positive
enthalpy change).

children.title: Know that if a reversible reaction is exothermic in one direction, it is endothermic in the
children.text: other direction.

children.title: Be able to interpret energy level diagrams.
Be able to calculate energy changes from specific heat capacities and changes in
children.text: temperature in calorimetry experiments.

children.title: Know that bond breaking is endothermic and bond formation is exothermic, and be able
children.text:
to use bond energy data to calculate energy changes.
C12. Electrolysis

children.title: Understand the terms electrode, cathode (negative electrode), anode (positive electrode)
children.text: and electrolyte.

children.title: Understand why direct current (dc), and not alternating current (ac), is used in
children.text: electrolysis.

children.title: Understand that in electrolysis at the cathode, the cations (positively charged ions)
children.text:
receive electrons (reduction) to change into atoms or molecules, and at the anode, the
anions (negatively charged ions) lose electrons to form atoms or molecules (oxidation).

children.title: Understand and be able to predict the products of the electrolysis of the following:

children.label: a
children.text:
aqueous solutions (including those of salts), including situations where more than
one ion/molecule is attracted to a single electrode

children.label: b
children.text: molten binary compounds

children.title: Be able to write half-equations for the processes taking place at each electrode.
Explain how electrolysis is used to electroplate objects.
children.text: C13. Carbon/Organic chemistry

children.title: General concepts:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know that crude oil is the main source of hydrocarbons and that it is separated
into fractions by fractional distillation (names and uses of specific fractions not
expected).

children.label: b
children.text:
Understand the link between carbon chain length and the following trends in
physical properties of hydrocarbons: boiling points, viscosity, flammability.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know the use of longer chain alkanes in cracking to form shorter chain alkanes
and alkenes, and be able to write balanced chemical equations for these
reactions.

children.label: d
children.text: Understand structural isomerism and be able to recognise examples.

children.label: e
children.text:
Understand and be able to use the following terms: molecular formula,
full structural formula (displayed structure) and condensed structural formula.

children.label: f
children.text:
Understand and be able to use the terms complete combustion and incomplete
combustion, and be able to write balanced chemical equations for such
reactions.

children.label: g
children.text:
Know the IUPAC guidelines for the systematic naming of carbon compounds,
and apply the guidelines in order to be able to name all the compounds in this
section of the specification.

children.label: h
children.text: Know and understand the terms homologous series and functional group.

children.title: Alkanes (saturated hydrocarbons):

children.label: a
children.text: Describe alkanes as a homologous series with the general formula of CnH2n +2.

children.label: b
children.text:
Be able to name, or recognise from the name, the C1 to C6 straight-chain
alkanes.

children.title: Alkenes (unsaturated hydrocarbons):

children.label: a
children.text:
Describe alkenes as a homologous series with a double bond and the general
formula CnH2n.

children.label: b
children.text:
Be able to name, or recognise from the name, C2 to C6 straight-chain alkenes,
including the position of the double bond.

children.label: c
children.text: Recognise and be able to use the test for unsaturation with bromine water.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know that addition reactions take place with the following substances: hydrogen,
halogens, hydrogen halides and steam. Be able to write the balanced chemical
equations for these reactions and recognise the formulae of the products formed.
(Mechanisms and consideration of carbocation stability are not required.)

children.title: Polymers:

children.label: a
children.text: Addition polymerisation, polyalkenes:

children.label: i
children.text:
Know that alkenes or other molecules with a C=C bond may react with
each other to form long-chain saturated molecules called polymers by
addition reactions called polymerisation, and that the unsaturated
molecules are called monomers.

children.label: ii
children.text:
If given an unsaturated monomer molecule, be able to recognise the
structure of the polymer and vice versa.

children.label: iii
children.text: Be able to recognise the repeating unit of these polymers.

children.label: b
children.text:
Condensation polymerisation, polyesters and polyamides (to include amino acids
forming proteins):

children.label: i
children.text:
If given the monomer molecules, be able to recognise the structure of the
polymer and vice versa.

children.label: ii
children.text: Be able to recognise the repeating unit of these polymers.

children.label: c
children.text:
Understand the terms biodegradable and non-biodegradable when applied to
polymers.

children.title: Alcohols:

children.label: a
children.text: Describe alcohols as a homologous series with the general formula CnH2n +1OH.

children.label: b
children.text:
Be able to name, or recognise from the name, C1 to C6 straight-chain alcohols,
including the position of the -OH group.

children.label: c
children.text: Describe the reaction of alcohols with sodium metal.

children.title: Carboxylic acids:

children.label: a
children.text:
Describe carboxylic acids as a homologous series with the general formula
CnH2n +1COOH.

children.label: b
children.text:
Be able to name, or recognise from the name, C1 to C6 straight-chain carboxylic
acids.

children.label: c
children.text:
Describe the chemical properties of carboxylic acids as those of weak acids, and
so be able to predict their reactions and determine the formulae of their salts.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know that carboxylic acids react with alcohols in the presence of an acid catalyst
to produce esters.
C14. Metals

children.title: Understand that the reactivity of a metal is linked to its tendency to form positive ions and
children.text: the ease of extraction of the metal.

children.title: Be able to use displacement reactions to establish the order of reactivity of metals and
children.text: vice versa.

children.title: Describe how the uses of metals are related to their physical and chemical properties,

children.label: e
children.text:
g. Al, Fe, Cu, Ag, Au, Ti, and understand that alloys can be formed to produce materials
with specific properties.

children.title: Know that most metal ores are the oxides of the metal, and that the extraction of metals
children.text: always involves reduction processes.

children.title: Know that common properties of transition metals include:

children.label: a
children.text: they are able to form stable ions in different oxidation states

children.label: b
children.text: they often form coloured compounds

children.label: c
children.text:
they are often used as catalysts (as ions or atoms)
C15. Kinetic/Particle theory

children.title: Be able to describe the packing and movement of particles in the three states of matter:
children.text: solid, liquid and gas.

children.title: Understand the changes to the packing and movement of particles in the following
children.text:
changes of state: freezing, melting, boiling/evaporating, and condensing. Understand that
the energy required for these processes is related to the bonding and structure of the
substance, including a consideration of intermolecular forces.
C16. Chemical tests

children.title: Know and recognise the following tests for gases:

children.label: a
children.text:
hydrogen – explodes with a ‘squeaky pop’ when a burning splint is held at the
open end of a test tube

children.label: b
children.text: oxygen – relights a glowing splint

children.label: c
children.text: carbon dioxide – limewater turns cloudy when shaken with the gas

children.label: d
children.text:
chlorine – damp blue litmus paper turns red and then is bleached (paper turns
white)

children.title: Know, recognise and describe the following tests for the anions:

children.label: a
children.text: carbonates – using a dilute acid

children.label: b
c
children.text:
sulfates – using an aqueous solution of barium chloride in the presence of dilute
hydrochloric acid

children.title: Know and recognise the test for the following metal cations using aqueous sodium

children.label: a
children.text:
Al
3+, Ca2+ and Mg2+ each form a white precipitate.

children.label: b
children.text: Cu2+ forms a blue precipitate.

children.label: c
children.text: Fe2+ forms a green precipitate.

children.label: d
children.text: Fe3+ forms a brown precipitate.
hydroxide:

children.title: Recall and recognise the flame test for the cations of the following metals:
children.text: Li (crimson red), Na (yellow-orange), K (lilac), Ca (red-orange), Cu (green)

children.title: Know and recognise the test for the presence of water using anhydrous copper(II) sulfate
children.text:
(colour change from white to blue).
C17. Air and water

children.title: Know and be able to use the composition of dry air, and understand that fractional
children.text: distillation can be used to separate the components of air.

children.title: Know the origins and describe the effects of greenhouse gases such as CO2 and CH4.
Know the origins and effects of gaseous pollutants such as CO, CO2, SO2 and NOx.
Know the purpose of chlorine and fluoride ions in the treatment of drinking water.
PHYSICS

children.text: P1. Electricity

children.title: Electrostatics:

children.label: a
children.text: Know and understand that insulators can be charged by friction.

children.label: b
children.text: Know and understand that charging is caused by gain or loss of electrons.

children.label: c
children.text: Know and understand that like charges repel and unlike charges attract.

children.label: d
children.text:
Understand applications and hazards associated with electrostatics, including the
role of earthing.

children.title: Electric circuits:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and recognise the basic circuit symbols and diagrams, including: cell,
battery, light source, resistor, variable resistor, ammeter, voltmeter, switch,
diode.

children.label: b
children.text:
Understand the difference between alternating current (ac) and direct current
(dc).

children.label: c
children.text:
Understand the difference between conductors and insulators, and recall
examples of each type.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know and be able to apply: current =
charge
time , I = Q
t

children.label: e
children.text: Know and understand the use of voltmeters and ammeters.

P2. Magnetism

children.title: Properties of magnets:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and be able to use the terms north pole, south pole, attraction and
repulsion.

children.label: b
children.text: Know the magnetic field pattern around a bar magnet (including direction).

children.label: c
children.text:
Understand the difference between soft and hard magnetic materials (e.g. iron
and steel).

children.label: d
children.text: Qualitatively understand induced magnetism.

children.title: Magnetic field due to an electric current:

children.label: a
children.text: Know and understand the magnetic effect of a current.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know the magnetic field patterns around current-carrying wires (including
direction) for straight wires and coils/solenoids.

children.label: c
children.text: Know and understand the factors affecting magnetic field strength around a wire.

children.label: d
children.text: Understand the difference between permanent magnets and electromagnets.

children.title: The motor effect:

children.label: a
children.text: Know that a wire carrying a current in a magnetic field can experience a force.

children.label: b
children.text:
Know the factors affecting the direction of a force on a wire in a magnetic field
(including the left-hand rule).

children.label: c
children.text:
Know the factors affecting the magnitude of the force on a wire in a magnetic
field.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know and be able to apply F=BIL for a straight wire at right angles to a uniform
magnetic field.

children.label: e
children.text:
Know and understand the construction and operation of a dc motor, including
factors affecting the magnitude of the force produced.

children.label: f
children.text: Understand applications of electromagnets.

children.title: Electromagnetic induction:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand that a voltage is induced when a wire cuts magnetic field
lines, or when a magnetic field changes.

children.label: b
children.text: Know the factors affecting the magnitude of an induced voltage.

children.label: c
children.text: Know the factors affecting the direction of an induced voltage.

children.label: d
children.text:
Understand the operation of an ac generator, including factors affecting the
output voltage.

children.label: e
children.text:
Interpret the graphical representation of the output voltage of a simple ac
generator.

children.label: f
children.text: Understand applications of electromagnetic induction.

children.label: a

Understand power transmission, including calculating losses during transmission

children.label: c
children.text: Know and be able to apply: force = rate of change of momentum

children.label: a

children.label: b

children.label: a
children.text: Understand and be able to apply the effect of temperature on density of fluid.

children.label: a
children.text: Understand thermal radiation as electromagnetic waves in the infrared region.

children.label: b
children.text: Know and be able to apply absorption and emission of radiation.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and be able to apply factors affecting rate of absorption and emission of
thermal radiation.

children.text:
Know and be able to explain properties of solids, liquids and gases in terms of

children.text:

children.text:

children.text:

children.label: c

children.label: a
children.text: Understand the transfer of energy without net movement of matter.

children.label: b
children.text: Know and understand transverse and longitudinal waves.

children.label: c
children.text: Know and understand the terms: peak, trough, compression and rarefaction.

children.label: d
children.text: Recall examples of waves, including electromagnetic waves and sound.

children.text:

children.label: a
children.text: Know and understand reflection at a surface.

children.label: b
children.text: Know and understand refraction at a boundary.

children.label: c
children.text:
Know and understand the effect of reflection and refraction on the speed,
frequency, wavelength and direction of waves.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know and understand the analogy of reflection and refraction of light with that of
water waves.

children.label: e
children.text: Know and understand the Doppler effect.

children.title: Optics:

children.label: a
children.text: Draw and interpret ray diagrams to describe reflection in plane mirrors.

children.label: b
children.text: Know and be able to apply: angle of incidence = angle of reflection

children.label: c
children.text: Draw and interpret ray diagrams for refraction at a planar boundary.

children.label: d
children.text: Know and be able to interpret angle of incidence and angle of refraction.

children.label: e
children.text:
Know and understand the effect of refraction on wave direction (away from or
towards the normal) and speed (increasing or decreasing).

children.title: Sound waves:

children.label: a
children.text: Understand the production of sound waves by a vibrating source.

children.label: b
children.text: Understand the need for a medium.

children.label: c
children.text:
Understand qualitatively the relation of loudness to amplitude and pitch to
frequency.

children.label: d
children.text: Know and understand longitudinal waves.

children.label: e
children.text: Understand that reflection causes echoes.

children.label: f
children.text: Recall that the range of human hearing is 20 Hz to 20 kHz.

children.label: g
children.text: Know and understand ultrasound and its uses (sonar and medical scanning).

children.title: Electromagnetic spectrum:

children.label: a
children.text:
Know and understand the nature and properties of electromagnetic waves (they
are transverse waves and travel at the speed of light in a vacuum).

children.label: b
children.text:
Recall the component parts of the spectrum (radio waves, microwaves, IR,
visible light, UV, X-rays, gamma).

children.label: c
children.text:
Understand the distinction of the component parts by different wavelengths
and/or frequencies.

children.label: d
children.text: Recall the order of the component parts by wavelength and/or frequency.

children.label: e
children.text:
Understand applications and hazards of the component parts of the
electromagnetic spectrum.
P7. Radioactivity

children.title: Atomic structure:

children.label: a
children.text: Understand the atom in terms of protons, neutrons and electrons.

children.label: b
children.text: Know and be able to apply the nuclear model of atomic structure.

children.label: c
children.text: Know the relative charges and masses of protons, neutrons and electrons.

children.label: d
children.text: Understand and be able to use the terms atomic number and mass number.

children.label: e
children.text: Know and understand the term isotope.

children.label: f
children.text: Know and understand the term nuclide, and use nuclide notation.

children.label: g
children.text: Understand that ionisation is caused by the gain/loss of electrons.

children.title: Radioactive decay:

children.label: a
children.text: Know and understand that emissions arise from an unstable nucleus.

children.label: b
children.text: Know and understand the random nature of emissions.

children.label: c
children.text: Know and understand the differences between alpha, beta and gamma emission.

children.label: d
children.text:
Know and understand the nature of alpha and beta particles, and gamma
radiation.

children.label: e
children.text: Be able to use and interpret nuclear equations.

children.label: f
children.text: Know the effect of decay on atomic number and mass number.

children.title: Ionising radiation:

children.label: a
children.text: Know the relative penetrating abilities of alpha, beta and gamma radiation.

children.label: b
children.text: Know the relative ionising abilities of alpha, beta and gamma radiation.

children.label: c
children.text:
Understand qualitatively the deflection of alpha, beta and gamma radiation in
electric or magnetic fields.

children.label: d
children.text: Know and appreciate the existence of background radiation.

children.label: e
children.text: Understand the applications and hazards of ionising radiation.

children.title: Half-life:

children.label: a
children.text:
Be able to interpret graphical representations of radioactive decay (including
consideration of decay products).

children.label: b
children.text: Understand the meaning of the term half-life.

children.label: c
children.text: Understand and be able to apply half-life calculations.
